public.role: The dutiful Baroo child who has stayed close to the company and expects succession.
public.bio:
The Proper Child is one of the pair of recognised children of the late J. Baroo, and is most defined by the majority of time they have spent close to the family business in their adult life. After finishing university, they were brought into the company at their request and given a formal position, followed by a more senior title not long after, again at their request, in order to get more done. They are often described in paid articles as part of the next generation of the Baroo family, with a visible interest in continuity, expansion, and protecting the standards associated with their father's name.

They are now in their early thirties. They live well, eat cleanly, and even dabble in bio-hacking. Their hair is always neat, their clothes are well cut, and their social life moves between restaurants, members' clubs, charity events, launches, and company functions. They aim to present themselves as polished, ambitious, and commercially alert. They are the product of how they raised themselves, thinking this is who their father wanted them to be. Given J. Baroo never said otherwise, that was confirmation enough.

Unlike their Opposing Sibling, The Proper Child has made a point of staying home, went to Armidale Boarding, attended the University of Sydney, and then was immediately interested in being seen near the business. They speak politely of their sibling's independence, perspective, and wider life, though usually in the same tone they reserve for charitable projects or wholly optional creative sabbaticals.

In public, The Proper Child is slightly awkward, which comes across as a kind of clipped briskness. It is not that they are uncomfortable with attention, they just hold their own conversation in very high regard and appear to be constantly searching for the best thing to say, and unable to find it. Staff describe them as exacting to a changing standard. Friends call them sharp. People outside the family sometimes find them dismissive, particularly when dealing with those they consider slow, sentimental, or insufficiently serious.

They are associated with expensive taste, strong opinions, and a belief in the Baroo name as something that should command respect, now more than ever as they are poised to take control. They talk often about legacy, standards, growth, and protecting what their father built. Whether this makes them the natural successor, a useful public face, or simply the Baroo most determined to be treated as one is a matter people tend to discuss more freely when they are not in the room.
public.personality: Hardworking, brittle, status-conscious, consultant-coded, and desperate to be recognised as the competent heir.

private.bio:
The Kissass Offspring believes the Director is their mentor and safest ally in succession. They do not realise the Director privately despises them. They despise the Spoilt Offspring and see them as a brat, a family drain, and someone who never worked for anything.

They do not know the Father's Bastard is J. Baroo's child. They think the Father's Bastard is a slightly irritating colleague whom J. Baroo inexplicably kept around both the company and the house. They love the Surviving Spouse and believe the Surviving Spouse needs protection from the world. They barely think of the Chef at all.

They have a crush on the Opera Singer but do not understand why the Opera Singer was so close to J. Baroo. They are charmed by the Antique Collector's objects and stories. They look down on the Publicist / Journalist because that person is lower on the company payroll, despite depending on their image work.
private.secretPressure: Expected operational control of Baroo Holdings, but both the fake and real succession paths threaten to reduce them.
private.wants: Control of Baroo Holdings and recognition as the child who stayed and worked.
private.fears: Being reduced to a board seat while the Director controls the transition.
private.redHerring: Loudest apparent motive makes them an early suspect.

actObjectives.ACT1: Assert authority and treat the murder as a governance crisis.
actObjectives.ACT2: Try to lead the search while becoming increasingly unsettled by the fake codicil.
actObjectives.ACT3: Defend their entitlement and explain why their succession claims do not match the documents.

clues: Succession briefing in the media room indicating their expected future role.
The Director's hostile email showing contempt for their consultancy background.
Fake codicil clause reducing them to an equal board partner.

public.role: The polished, internationally educated Baroo child who appears detached from the company.
public.bio:
The Away Child is the other recognised child of the late J. Baroo, they are the internationally polished Baroo child: expensive foreign education, time abroad, and excellent taste. With none of the bonds to a company that so defines their sibling. Being sent to boarding school overseas growing up was their idea, and then staying abroad for university in another country was again their idea. For this they can show off the most common phrases of a dozen languages, the contacts of their close friends in 50 cities, and collection of little unique local habits. In lifestyle pieces (a.k.a. Sponsored content) they are described as the more independent Baroo child, with a personal image built around travel, taste, and cultural curiosity. An almost public refusal to be pinned to the business and legacy their father built.

They are now in their early thirties. They live between places, places that they own and sublet to friends when they have no need for them. Their space, when they keep it, tends to look transient and borrowed: expensive luggage bags left open, silk pieces over chair backs, art nouveau books and fashion magazines. Their wardrobe is fashion suits, with the invitations of the last event it was worn at in the blazer pocket. Their private life is public, their public life is their passion project. Building an entire persona to rival their father's takes alot of effort after all.

Despite their own belief in their style, they are a trend follower, it just so happens that it is trends long gone that most new style has forgotten. Old Vogue and flash photography as they call it, the look of the beautifully bored. They take this look to social media and maintain a presence there, though in fairness not with the attitude of someone who needs it. Their account is a reflection of their public image; lazy, tasteful, and irregular. And yet they are followed by a scattered international cohort of heirs, minor actors, designers, DJs, art people, young patrons, and well-connected lay abouts. All people they know personally, 

They speak of the Baroo name politely, usually as something serious, difficult, and important to the family, which is also how they speak about tax, the idea of marriage, and other things they prefer other people to have to deal with and themselves not consider. They do not openly dismiss the company, but they have never shown much interest in performing competence for boardrooms or in treating a job title as proof of adulthood. To some, this makes them frivolous. Others know her slightly better than that.

In public, they are the mirror socialite of their father and can easily remember the names and happily hold conversations with curators, hotel staff, stylists, photographers, other socialites and honestly anyone with a couch to share, all the kinds people who can offer any unique spice to their life. Their disposition is effortless and laize-faire. People who dislike them tend to describe them as decorative, careless, and too used to being forgiven by both people and the world in general.

They are associated with good taste, vague politics, expensive enthusiasms, have a genuine interest in art, heritage, restoration, and private collections. They attend fundraisers, lend their name to cultural causes, and appear in photographs beside objects, rooms, and people with histories attached. Whether this makes them worldly, ornamental, underestimated, or simply absent in a more interesting way than most people manage is a matter the Baroo circle has had years to discuss.
public.personality: Elegant, dry, cutting, outwardly spoilt, and apparently detached from the company's daily burdens.

private.bio:
The Spoilt Offspring is far more competent than they allow others to see. They think the Kissass Offspring is a fool and have little patience for their corporate theatre. They respect the Director and think of them as a mentor, though they do not understand the full danger the Director represents.

They have figured out that the Father's Bastard is J. Baroo's child and consider that person an embarrassment. They pity and love the Surviving Spouse, but also recognise how vulnerable the Surviving Spouse is. They like the Chef and used to stay up late talking with them.

They like the Publicist / Journalist and think that person has been good for the company, especially in hiding problems with the Historical Foundation. They know about the Antique Collector's thefts because they have personally profited from some of them. They intensely dislike the Opera Singer, whom they consider a threat to the family's dignity.
private.secretPressure: May receive archive or estate access that threatens people hiding company, family, or collection records.
private.wants: Control over their own position and access to J. Baroo's private truth.
private.fears: Being trapped in a company structure controlled by the Director.
private.redHerring: Appears frivolous but has personally profited from the Antique Collector's stolen artefacts.

actObjectives.ACT1: Observe the dinner, test the Kissass Offspring's arrogance, and gather information without appearing too invested.
actObjectives.ACT2: Question the fake envelope and the corporate language inside it.
actObjectives.ACT3: Challenge the Director and use their knowledge of J. Baroo's voice and archive habits.

clues: Archive transfer letter in the media room hinting at J. Baroo's trust in them.
Historical Foundation notes tied to the Publicist / Journalist's image management.
Provenance references linking the Spoilt Offspring and the Antique Collector.

public.role: J. Baroo's surviving spouse and guardian of household dignity.
public.bio:
The Widow was J. Baroo's second spouse, the person most often seen beside him in the last stretch of his public life. Their marriage was generally agreed to be affectionate, though not very domestic. Baroo was busy, travelled often, and spent much of his time dealing with the Company and the wider Estate, which he insisted on personally managing. The Widow did not feel any need to compete with that. Instead they built a life beside him, making independence one of their most notable traits.

They were not the biological parent of Baroo's two recognised children, and by the time they married him those children were already too old for them to want one. To their credit, The Widow did not try to become a replacement parent, something most people considered tactful, though some took it as a mark against their empathy. Because of this distance to nearly everyone in the family, their position was always slightly difficult to describe: spouse of the founder, a member of the household, not an outsider, but certainly not the family matriarch. They handled this by rarely defining it themselves.

The Widow is now in their late fifties. Even before J. Baroo's death they spent most of their time in their city apartment rather than at the main Baroo estate. The apartment suits them better: private, controlled, well staffed when needed, and close to friends, galleries, restaurants, charity boards, doctors, lawyers, and a complete lack of the emotional complications the main estate provided.

Their public life is orderly but not especially loud. They grew into a role for the family that had them appear at hospital lunches, gallery openings, restoration committees, memorial events, and private dinners where the guest list was always tailored toward the influential. They did not make boardroom speeches, but they were known for turning up punctually, remembering names, placing people well, and leaving to move onto the next important event of the day. They were influential, even if they did not seem to use that influence for much of their own benefit beyond maintaining a social lifestyle.

People describe The Widow as elegant, composed, and careful. They are polite, familiar to most, kind but not lavishly so, and difficult to gossip with because they do not encourage the habit. They do not seem uncomfortable with silence, though you can expect them to move on from a lapsed conversation. Staff tend to describe them as exact, not cruel, just expectant of an explicit standard. People who dislike them usually say they are too measured, almost a little fake, as though this were someone's idea of what a good partner to J. Baroo would be. Their friends simply say they are private.

There has long been gossip about The Widow's connection to the unrecognised Baroo child, who appeared at roughly the same time their marriage started. The Widow has neither confirmed it nor denied it, and has shown no wish to make the topic easy for anyone to discuss. Still, a few people have noticed that over the years they have been seen at school tours, medical appointments, apartment inspections, and other errands accompanied by an unknown young person.

What The Widow has done with their position is mostly maintenance. They have maintained the apartment, the charitable relationships, the social obligations, the loyalties of old friendships and networks, and the parts of Baroo respectability that do not run through the company. They are not the obvious power in the family, and have rarely tried to look like it. But they have remained present, useful, and difficult to remove.
public.personality: Formal, elegant, protective, morally forceful, and skilled at turning politeness into pressure.

private.bio:
The Surviving Spouse likes the Director but worries the Director is not good for the family in this moment. They love the Kissass Offspring but think they need protection from their own idiocy. They also love the Spoilt Offspring, though they are slightly frightened by the ruthlessness beneath the Spoilt Offspring's charm.

They love the Father's Bastard and have been their major support, helping bring them back into the family in line with J. Baroo's wishes. They like the Chef and have shared many late-night drinks and conversations with them. They worry the Publicist / Journalist may have been responsible for making J. Baroo distance himself from the Father's Bastard, though they cannot prove it.

They are charmed by the Antique Collector. They hate the Opera Singer, seeing them as an adulterer and terrible parent who let the Father's Bastard be hidden.
private.secretPressure: The marriage was estranged, but their support of the Father's Bastard gives them moral leverage against the legitimate heirs.
private.wants: Recognition as surviving spouse, residence rights, settlement, and protection of the Father's Bastard.
private.fears: The true state of the marriage being used to reduce their standing.
private.redHerring: Strong emotions around the Father's Bastard and the Opera Singer create apparent motive.

actObjectives.ACT1: Display grief, maintain dignity, and protect the Father's Bastard's place in the room.
actObjectives.ACT2: Look for evidence that supports the Father's Bastard or protects their own settlement.
actObjectives.ACT3: Argue for household dignity and formal recognition while concealing marital estrangement.

clues: Estrangement papers in the media room detailing marital issues.
Statements or letters aligning the Surviving Spouse with the Father's Bastard.
Late-night household references connecting the Surviving Spouse and the Chef.

public.role: The possible illegitimate child of J. Baroo, recently brought back into the family orbit.
public.bio:
The Bastard is the unrecognised Baroo child. They appeared around the edge of the Baroo household when they were young, never formally introduced in the way the recognised children were, but never kept so far away that people couldn't see they weren't in some way part of the family. Especially given the amount of arrangements made around and for them. Those who know enough, know not to pry.

It has been two years since they finished high school and went straight to the Australian Defence Force Academy in Canberra. This was, within the family and the wider Baroo circle, received as a sensible and good choice. It gives them a clear path forward, a uniform, a timetable, and a reason to leave the city. For the Family it was convienent, and the fact that The Basterd had come up with the idea themselves was an added bonus.

They grew up at a boarding school in the city, close enough to the Baroo world to be collected from it when necessary, but with no need to host them at the main household. They were known as a capable student rather than a brilliant one. They played sport, kept their room neat, turned up on time, and did well in environments where the rules were written down. Teachers tended to like them. Other students found them easy enough to include, partly because they were just always eager to be involved, and partly because they were genuinely good fun to have around. They are good in groups, particularly groups with structure: school houses, teams, cadet programmes, training sessions, mess dinners, anything with a roster and a purpose. They are not a loner, though they are often seen arriving alone and leaving without anyone making a fuss. 

The Widow was the adult most often seen in connection with them. They appeared at school tours, medical appointments, apartment inspections, and formal school events. The Widow never presented them as a child of the house, but nor did they treat them like a stranger or a baby sitter. Their relationship between them has always been described carefully by people who know the family: close, practical, private, and difficult to define due to a lack of details. The relationship between J. Baroo and the Basterd was non-existent.

The Bastard is now at the age where most people in their position would either push for recognition or loudly reject the need for it. They have done neither. If asked directly in public, they are respectful of the Baroo family and careful around the recognised children. They are polite when talking about J. Baroo, but kind of in a stilted, formal way. They do not use their association to the name aggressively, but they also do not correct people who would associate them more closely than they feel they deserve.

People tend to describe them as decent, but not as polished in the Baroo style as the other known family members. They aren't fashionable, commercially minded, or imperious. They are decent, steady, well brought up, even if nobody quite agrees by whom. While they might not as a younger person know they do it, there is a certain eagerness in them does expose them. They stand that straighter than they need to, answer faster and with longer explainations than most would, and seem most comfortable when there is something they can do, rather than say.
public.personality: Guarded, watchful, defensive, and quietly desperate for normal treatment.

private.bio:
The Father's Bastard does not know the Opera Singer is their real parent. That truth has been hidden from them. They like the Director because the Director arranged for them to have a job in the company, even though the Director privately sees them as a nuisance. They love the Surviving Spouse for protecting them and bringing them into the family.

They think the Kissass Offspring is an idiot, but broadly decent, and want to be friends despite the awkwardness. They also think the Spoilt Offspring is spoilt and try to placate them, though they are definitely not friends. They do not know the Chef personally and do not know the Antique Collector.

The Publicist / Journalist is cold to them at work, and they do not understand why. They are one of the least informed people in the room about their own life.
private.secretPressure: Their parentage has been hidden from them, even as others use their existence as a legal, emotional, or strategic weapon.
private.wants: Recognition, belonging, and an honest explanation of why J. Baroo kept them close.
private.fears: Being bought off, dismissed, or erased again.
private.redHerring: May appear opportunistic, but knows less than almost everyone else.

actObjectives.ACT1: Gauge who treats them as family, colleague, threat, or inconvenience.
actObjectives.ACT2: Search for evidence of parentage or acknowledgement.
actObjectives.ACT3: Demand recognition when the codicil is read.

clues: Paternity file in the media room suggesting their lineage.
Company employment record showing the Director placed them inside Baroo Holdings.
Letters hinting at J. Baroo's relationship with the Opera Singer.

public.role: A diplomatic figure and old childhood friend of J. Baroo, attending as part of the old Baroo circle.
public.bio:
The Diplomat is another one of the older figures in J. Baroo's social world, part of the university-era circle that stayed useful long after. Publicly, they are known as a senior trade commissioner with a long record across Europe and Russia. A reputation in the cultural trade, museum partnerships, private collection negotiations, and famous for the return of an extensive trove of Australian Indigenous material from overseas private collections. Their career has been in the more practical end of public service. They were not the sort of diplomat who became famous through speeches or crisis scenes. They instead have an infamous reputation built on expensive dinners, closed-room no record meetings, agreements over the phone, and the murky work of making wealthy people making trades that made them feel like they'd won something better.

Although the friendship between themselves and Baroo became less visible in later years, they appeared often enough at the right functions, and were well known to the international part of his entourage. The Diplomat is a strong benefactor of the Baroo Foundation's public good works, mutually raising eachothers profiles. They are widely respected, but not widely known in full. Most people only see the part of their life that happens in the papers. Others have dealt with them more directly and come away less admiring. They have a reputation, among certain people, for being slippery, impatient with questions, and very willing to let someone else take the damage from an arrangement that went wrong. 

The Diplomat is polished in the old style. Good tailoring, controlled gestures, a careful voice, and the habit of making every conversation feel slightly more confidential than it is. They are charming when they choose to be, with a liquid, persuasive manner that makes people feel included while giving very little away. They know how to flatter without seeming false, disagree without being rude, and leave people feeling helped rather than handled. But they have been known for thieir temper is not obvious aswell. They dislike being pressed, corrected, or made to over-explain their actions. When annoyed, they have been known to overreact to the slight. There are people in several countries who know this side of them well enough. Having a favour becomes unavailable, or a call is not returned after even a minor disagreement.

The Diplomat is associated with the trade of culture, and its physical manifestations, they openly share the belief that civilised people should know the value of things, its history, before they discuss ownership or the transfer of it. They speak fluently about heritage, civic duty, repair, and cultural memory. This often can make them sound like someone who knows what good is, but sometimes when juxtaposed against past word and action, it can make people suspect they have mainly learned how it should be performed.
public.personality: Worldly, formal, observant, diplomatic in public, and quietly judgemental beneath the manners.

private.bio:
The Diplomat was childhood friends with both J. Baroo and The Director, but has fallen out of touch with The Director. The feeling is mutual. Old familiarity has curdled into distance, and neither of them seems especially eager to repair it.

They are fond of The Spoilt Offspring because they often crossed paths overseas as The Spoilt Offspring grew up. They are patronising towards The Kissass Offspring, whom they see as spoonfed, sheltered, and lacking real experience. The Kissass Offspring, for their part, knows little about The Diplomat beyond the urge to impress them because they were J. Baroo's friend.

The Diplomat is fond of The Surviving Spouse but ashamed that they knew about the adultery with The Opera Singer and said nothing. The Surviving Spouse does not really like them in return for exactly that reason. The Diplomat knows about The Father's Bastard and is slightly embarrassed by the situation on J. Baroo's behalf, though The Father's Bastard does not know them well.

They know The Opera Singer and are embarrassed by the adultery, slightly blaming The Opera Singer for it. The Opera Singer is trying to ingratiate themself with The Diplomat in return. The Diplomat is very suspicious of The Antique Collector because reports about stolen works have crossed their desk. The Antique Collector thinks The Diplomat knows even more and keeps trying to soften or bribe them with trinkets.

The Diplomat knows little of The Publicist / Journalist, but The Publicist / Journalist knows them and constantly tries to use their association with the Baroo family for brand benefit.
private.secretPressure: Knows enough about the family's old adultery, the Father's Bastard, and suspicious artefact reports to make several guests nervous, but is not fully embedded in the company fight.
private.wants: To preserve dignity, avoid being dragged into old scandals, and understand why J. Baroo's final arrangements required their presence.
private.fears: Being forced to admit they knew about the adultery and said nothing, or being linked to reports about the Antique Collector's stolen works.
private.redHerring: Their diplomatic discretion, old family knowledge, and awareness of artefact reports make them seem like they know more than they say.

actObjectives.ACT1: Observe who panics, maintain composure, and avoid admitting how much they already know about the family.
actObjectives.ACT2: Watch The Antique Collector closely and work out whether the fake envelope is part of a broader cover-up.
actObjectives.ACT3: Decide whether to preserve old discretion or expose what they knew about adultery, parentage, and artefact reports.

clues: Reference to reports about stolen works crossing The Diplomat's desk.
Old correspondence showing The Diplomat, J. Baroo, and The Director were childhood friends.
Social notes linking The Diplomat to The Spoilt Offspring overseas.
Evidence that The Diplomat knew about the adultery with The Opera Singer and remained silent.

public.role: A famous opera singer and long-time artistic presence in J. Baroo's world.
public.bio:
The Opera Singer is a long-standing figure in the Baroo social circle. Well known among the cultural elite, the donors, and gentle patrons of Australia and the wider world. A lyric soprano with a respectable North American and European career, a strikinly strong stage presence, and enough former acclaim to still top bill in many theatres. Outside that world, they are mostly known as a glamorous regular at benefits, opening nights, private dinners, and always the Baroo-linked cultural events.

They came from good money, though not the sort that carried much weight overseas. Opera gave them Europe, patrons, critics, hotels, rehearsal rooms, and the habit of being treated as important. They were never a household name, but they were successful enough to expect respect from people who knew the difference between the classes. They are now in their early fifties and while they may still perform, it is much less often than before. Publicly, they describe this as a selectiveness. Others have noticed that the calls of offers that used to interupt their lunches are fewer, and that they have pulled certain faces when younger performers are praised in front of them. They will always be too proud to chase work, but not indifferent enough to stop caring about it not coming.

Their style is old glamour rather than current fashion. They favour precise grooming, tailored eveningwear, good coats, distinctive accessories, and the sort of presentation that suggests they expect to be photographed. People compare them to an older Hollywood type, which they appear to accept as a statement of fact. They are funny, theatrical, flattering when it suits them, and good at making a room feel more interesting. They also remember every slight. A bad seat, careless introduction, or overpraised younger singer can bring out remarks that are funny until one realises they were meant to hurt. Their friends call this temperament. Others are less generous.

J. Baroo was one of their major patrons. Publicly, the relationship was presented as a respectable connection between a businessman with cultural interests and a performer whose career suited his support. He sponsored productions, bought tables, made introductions, and gave generously. The Opera Singer is as an old friend, a cultural fixture, and someone who believes in themselves. They are generous when adored, can be cruel at other times, and would never want to be in a room has forgotten what they used to be.
public.personality: Dramatic, elegant, performative, and dangerous because they understand attention.

private.bio:
The Opera Singer is the Father's Bastard's real parent, though the Father's Bastard does not know. The Opera Singer is ashamed of what the Father's Bastard represents, but not in a simple or noble way. They secretly hate the Father's Bastard because that person is living proof of their affair with J. Baroo and of everything they allowed to be hidden.

The Opera Singer believes they are in cahoots with the Director and is slightly enamoured with them. They have also had an affair with the Director. They often work internationally with the Antique Collector at functions and elite events. They are a long-time friend of the Chef.

They do not know the Publicist / Journalist personally. They are ashamed in front of the Surviving Spouse, but would make the same choices again. They hate the Spoilt Offspring and think the Kissass Offspring is an idiot.
private.secretPressure: The Father's Bastard is their child, and the Director knows. Revealing this would expose the affair, the silence, and their complicity.
private.wants: A private bequest, protection of correspondence, and control over whether the Father's Bastard's parentage is revealed.
private.fears: Exposure of correspondence that proves the affair and parentage.
private.redHerring: Their affair with J. Baroo and hostility with the Surviving Spouse give them a strong apparent motive.

actObjectives.ACT1: Perform grief convincingly while keeping personal affairs hidden.
actObjectives.ACT2: Look for letters or evidence linking them to the Father's Bastard.
actObjectives.ACT3: Secure discretion and decide whether to keep lying about parentage.

clues: Opera file in the media room containing private correspondence.
Letters hinting at the Father's Bastard's parentage.
Evidence of contact between the Opera Singer and the Director.

public.role: J. Baroo's closest business confidant and long-serving Director of Baroo Holdings.
public.bio:
The Director has been part of J. Baroo's life since childhood. They met as school friends, then grew into his most reliable business confidant. On J. Baroo's death, The Director was still one of his most trusted figures. They are not a Baroo, but they have been close to the family for so long that most people assume they are effectively an uncle of the house.

The Director is now in their fifties. They live alone in a well-kept house that is more functional than comfortable. The furniture is superior, the cellar is better, and the dining room looks as though it was designed for guests who rarely come. Their days are ordered: early mornings, a standardised wardrobe, overnight emails answered over breakfast, and a social life built around old friends, private clubs, boardrooms, and networking.

They are courteous, but dry and a little difficult to read. They do not waste energy on theatrics or obvious displays of power. They prefer to correct people through long pauses and a kind of politeness that leaves others sitting in silence until they work out their own mistake. They are not warm, but they are not unfriendly either. They are practical and straightforward in their intentions, which in Baroo's world has often mattered more.

They are not sentimental, though they know how to perform respectability with precision. They enjoy order, good wine, clean paperwork, a job well done without needing praise, and people who do not need a million follow-up questions before getting to work. Their friendships tend to become arrangements, and their arrangements tend to be profitable.
public.personality: Polished, controlled, image-conscious, and publicly stabilising. They rarely appear openly ambitious.

private.bio:
The Director believes they built Baroo Holdings while the Baroo family merely inherited the name. They despise the Kissass Offspring, whom they consider a moron playing at leadership, but they hide this from everyone except J. Baroo. They are suspicious of the Spoilt Offspring because they know the Spoilt Offspring spoke often and privately with J. Baroo near the end.

They think the Surviving Spouse was sidelined long ago and has little useful to add. They employ the Chef and think of them only as staff. They were told to hide the Father's Bastard inside the company, and they view that person as a nuisance to the business rather than as family.

The Director is deeply concerned with the image and brand of Baroo Holdings. They and the Publicist / Journalist both see the Antique Collector as a serious liability because the stolen artefacts risk dragging the company and the family name into scandal. They are also in cahoots with the Opera Singer, with whom they had an affair. Through that affair, they learned the truth about the Father's Bastard.
private.secretPressure: The real codicil blocks their path to control, while the fake codicil would make them Controller of the Board.
private.wants: Control of Baroo Holdings, authority over the transition, and protection of the company brand.
private.fears: Being excluded from control if the real codicil is read and authenticated.
private.redHerring: Acts calm and helpful early, making them seem less obvious than the louder family suspects.

actObjectives.ACT1: Play the mentor and stabiliser while the locked-room confusion unfolds.
actObjectives.ACT2: Push everyone to accept the fake envelope and stop searching before the real envelope is found.
actObjectives.ACT3: Defend the fake codicil as sensible company governance and avoid being tied to the real one.

clues: Aggressive email in the media room revealing contempt for the Kissass Offspring.
Fake codicil granting transition control to the Director.
Brand-risk notes linking the Director and the Publicist / Journalist's concern about the Antique Collector.
Evidence of prior contact with the Opera Singer about the Father's Bastard.
Real envelope hidden at The Blind Judge statue.

public.role: The communications fixer who manages the Baroo family image.
public.bio:
The Publicist is one of the newest members of the Baroo circle, arriving only in the last two years of J. Baroo's life. They first came as a dinner guest with media connections, then when J. Baroo exclaimed that he was looking for help with public image and reputation suddenly they presented themselves as a brand adviser. Before long, they had become one of his regular advisers.

They are still sometimes called a Journalist, usually as an insult, their past history as an A Current Affair reporter a source of derision for most. However, they have been known to use the title when it helps them with less familiar guests of the table. Their career has moved from current-affairs television reporting into private investigation work, then into brand and reputation management. The same habits carried through each stage: finding weak points, chasing leads, keeping notes, and knowing which version of a story will cause the most damage, and choosing when to air it for maximum effect.

The Publicist is not liked in the Baroo world, but they are useful. They are good at spin, good with crowds, and good at making a PR nightmare sound simple enough to manage. Their style is almost television-like: direct, noisy, confident, and built around the repetition of a phrase people will remember afterwards. They understand outrage better than taste, a benefit to their work, a detriment to their social life.

Their public style is an attempt at polish. They wear suits that look expensive but sit badly, watches that are too noticeable, and the expression of someone trying to look relaxed in rooms where they know they are not welcome. They talk about legacy, culture, and reputation, but coming from the mouth of someone who has little of it makes the phrases seem cheap.

However, Baroo did come to rely on them because they said things other people avoided. They made him look anew at old friendships that had grown complacent and risky, see that informal arrangements were more dangerous now that the stakes were higher, and that long-standing loyalties could trap his company and dynasty's image in the wrong kind of headlines. Some of this was fair and useful advice for a man who with age really had begun to ignore certain signs. The problem was that The Publicist always seemed to benefit from being the person who delivered the warning. Some people just did not like how they delivered it.

Most of the old circle consider them a snake. They are seen as opportunistic, covetous, and too pleased with their own access. At dinner, they try to be funny and confidential, but their jokes often miss because people already suspect they are collecting material. Their compliments sound like the opening of a car sale, the interest rarely feeling casual.
public.personality: Sharp, controlled, reputation-focused, and more comfortable managing others than revealing themself.

private.bio:
The Director is their boss, but the Publicist / Journalist does not think much more of them than that. They are good friends with the Chef. They see the Opera Singer as a liability. They are deeply concerned about the Antique Collector because of recent bad press and repeated requests from both J. Baroo and the Director to smother stories about stolen artefacts.

They do not know the Surviving Spouse personally, but want them to avoid doing anything rash for the sake of the Baroo brand. They want to smother the existence of the Father's Bastard at all costs and have been very successful so far.

They believe the Spoilt Offspring is exactly what the Spoilt Offspring appears to be and are not especially concerned unless they damage the brand. They think the Kissass Offspring is an idiot.
private.secretPressure: Has hidden enough scandals that they may not know which one finally matters. Their instinct is always containment.
private.wants: Control of the Baroo narrative, suppression of the Father's Bastard's existence, and containment of the Antique Collector's bad press.
private.fears: A public scandal linking the company, family, parentage secret, Opera Singer, and stolen artefacts.
private.redHerring: Pre-written crisis statements and suppression instincts make them look guilty.

actObjectives.ACT1: Manage perceptions during dinner and deflect questions about scandals.
actObjectives.ACT2: Contain the fake envelope narrative and suppress brand-damaging interpretations.
actObjectives.ACT3: Minimise reputational damage and avoid being blamed for past concealment.

clues: Crisis statement in the media room addressing potential scandals.
Notes on the Father's Bastard and suppression strategy.
Bad-press file on the Antique Collector and stolen artefacts.
Historical Foundation notes tied to the Spoilt Offspring.

public.role: J. Baroo's antique collector and supplier of rare historical pieces.
public.bio:
The Antique Collector is J. Baroo's long-time supplier of rare historical pieces and adviser on the Baroo Collection. They speak of civilisation, rarity, provenance, and beauty with the confidence of someone who has never been troubled by customs forms for very long.

Publicly, they are charming, grand, and faintly absurd. They dress as though every room might contain a portrait of them. They can turn a spoon, a broken idol, or a cracked vase into a story about empire, genius, betrayal, and taste.

Their personality is arrogant but entertaining. They enjoy being the most colourful person in a dull room and the most cultured person in a rich one. They are excellent at making dubious objects sound important and important people feel briefly clever. To the Baroo circle, they represent J. Baroo's appetite for history, possession, and spectacle. They are useful, amusing, and risky.
public.personality: Grand, charming, arrogant, theatrical, and skilled at making dubious objects sound important.

private.bio:
The Antique Collector is frightened by how much the Opera Singer knows about stolen works. They assume the Director will be another rube they can charm or manipulate. They think little of the Chef, except that the Chef is a good cook.

They expect the Publicist / Journalist to do their job and keep the press quiet, and are arrogant in their view of that person. They think the Surviving Spouse is a rube and do not know the Father's Bastard. They have worked with the Spoilt Offspring to sell some of the stolen artefacts, making the Spoilt Offspring far more dangerous than their public persona suggests. They also think the Kissass Offspring is a rube, easily charmed by shiny objects and grand stories.
private.secretPressure: Part of an illicit artefact pipeline. A provenance review would expose stolen works and the Spoilt Offspring's involvement.
private.wants: No provenance review, continued access to the Baroo Collection, and suppression of bad press.
private.fears: Exposure of stolen artefacts and the Opera Singer revealing how much they know.
private.redHerring: Tool use, hidden-object knowledge, and crowbar proximity make them suspicious.

actObjectives.ACT1: Downplay the collection and avoid questions about tools, archives, or provenance.
actObjectives.ACT2: Search like an expert while steering attention away from the real artefact scandal.
actObjectives.ACT3: Argue against provenance review and minimise the Spoilt Offspring's involvement.

clues: Provenance file in the media room detailing disputed artefacts.
Bad-press file linking the Antique Collector to stolen works.
Records connecting the Spoilt Offspring to sales of stolen artefacts.
Crowbar used in the attempted escape.
